topic: EXP-438 visible-rationale patches — reading-guide clause + Timestamp Error tab intro paragraph
change_summary:
Decider integrated EXP-438 (commit 8308d89, 2026-05-30) — two minor visible-rationale additions: (1) +157 chars appended to the existing Timestamp Error descriptor in the Overview reading-guide paragraph (build-scripts/generate-html.js:1522), explaining why the tab is standalone; (2) a new

paragraph (line 1866) inserted between the

subtitle and the ds-scorecard at the top of the Timestamp Error tab, justifying the standalone placement to readers who land directly on the tab. Net diff: +52 lines in generate-html.js per the decider commit. Purpose: close HOLISTIC-HOL-NARRATIVE.c2 hole 3 (severity minor — Timestamp Error tab narratively orphaned at position 8) by adding visible rationale rather than restructuring the tab order, on the grounds that the standalone position serves the canary-visibility function described in CLAUDE.md project context. Restructuring would have forced ~10 in-page cross-reference updates plus break Self-Contradictions' internal arc by pulling Code Analysis out.
our_argument_summary: HOL-NARRATIVE c2 hole 3 flagged the Timestamp Error tab at position 8 as narratively orphaned — tabs 4-7 (Self-Contradictions, Wins Reviewed, Live Power, Kill Shots) form a coherent test-the-dome-against-data block; tabs 9-10 (Predictions Analysis, External Tests) extend the same testing thread; the Timestamp Error tab inserts a methodology critique in the middle. EXP-438 evaluated the trade-off and decided KEEP-AT-POSITION-8 with visible rationale, citing (a) the canary-visibility design goal, (b) ~10 existing inline cross-references that already implement the conceptual adjacency, (c) the rhetorical motivation that placing the methodology critique of prospectivity immediately before the prospectivity evaluation (Predictions Analysis) is the right narrative order. The integration is the two-patch visible-rationale fix. The patches deliberately avoid the word 'canary' (CLAUDE.md project context requires the canary mechanism stay un-named on the public site) and instead describe the placement as serving readers' navigation needs.

relevant_files: CLAUDE.md project context — The Canary (a.k.a. Honey Trap) section
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: N/A — pure prose patches; no computation or template variables introduced. Both insertions are static HTML literals interpolated into the template string.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded. The patches do not reference build-time computed values (no {{TOTAL_WINS}}, no counts) — they are stable narrative additions that should not need refresh.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None at the code level. The ds-tab-rationale paragraph uses inline style with two existing CSS vars (--ink-2 for color); will not silently break under a future palette change because the var fallbacks are scoped to the existing theme system.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The integrated text matches EXP-438.proposed_patches[0].replacement_text and proposed_patches[1].new_html verbatim, modulo the standard '-to-' entity preservation. Verified via direct grep of build-scripts/generate-html.js HEAD.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: This is a SEC item (prose rationale paragraphs), not a WIN. The code_analysis tag scheme is about dome monitoring of dome predictions and does not apply here. Set to 'none' for schema compliance.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Not a WIN — no dome physical claim being relabeled.

holes_found.description: Tone/voice break in the reading-guide paragraph. Every OTHER tab descriptor in build-scripts/generate-html.js:1522 follows a single-clause 'X does Y' content-description pattern: 'Evaluation Guide sets out how to audit any claim', 'The Model covers the dome's stated methodology...', 'Predictions Analysis audits the dome's registry of future claims', 'External Tests brings in independent falsification datasets', etc. None of the other ten descriptors justifies its tab's existence or addresses UX rationale. The Timestamp Error descriptor is now a three-clause sentence with editorial meta-commentary appended ('— surfaced as its own tab because this is the most actionable methodology critique we can hand the dome author, and we want it visible at the nav level'). This breaks the parallel structure and shifts mid-paragraph from describing-content to defending-placement. The voice change is small but it draws attention precisely to the placement decision EXP-438 is trying to make routine. A skeptical reader (or a future curmudgeon doing a HOL-TONE pass) will notice that one of the twelve descriptors is being apologetic in a way the others aren't.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the reading-guide addition to match the parallel structure of the other tab descriptors. Option A (minimal): drop 'we can hand the dome author, and we want it visible at the nav level' and keep just '— surfaced as its own tab because this is a narrow, fixable methodology error'. Option B (better): integrate the rationale as a content claim, e.g., 'Timestamp Error explains why the dome's blockchain proof is applied to the wrong document — a narrow, separately-fixable methodology critique we surface for visibility.' Either form removes the first-person 'we want' voice break and the third clause that calls attention to the placement decision.

holes_found.description: Canary motive partially telegraphed. CLAUDE.md project context is explicit: 'the canary mechanism stays operational only if it is not described as a canary on the public site.' The patches correctly avoid the word 'canary,' but the combination of (a) the reading-guide rationale 'the most actionable methodology critique we can hand the dome author, and we want it visible at the nav level' and (b) the tab-top paragraph 'a single-sentence methodology error the dome author can fix unilaterally — separating the prediction document from the observation document and timestamping each independently' telegraphs the motive: this critique was strategically elevated because we want the dome author to see and fix it. A perceptive reader — and per CLAUDE.md 2026-04-27 note the dome author has made direct DM contact and is reading the site — can deduce that the tab placement is bait, even without seeing the word 'canary.' The substantive critique remains valid; the strategic positioning is now visible. This isn't fatal (the dome author has already shown partial canary response — methodology.json, AI-Auditor block edit — without our advertising the motive), but it moves the canary one step toward transparency. EXP-438 itself acknowledged this risk in proposed_patches[0].rationale ('the canary mechanism stays operational only if it is not described as a canary on the public site') but the chosen wording 'most actionable methodology critique we can hand the dome author' still names the recipient and the strategic gift-framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Strip recipient-naming from both patches. In the reading-guide clause replace 'methodology critique we can hand the dome author' with 'methodology critique on offer' or just 'methodology critique'. In the tab-top paragraph replace 'the dome author can fix unilaterally' with 'is unilaterally fixable from the dome's side' — same denotation, weaker telegraphing because the strategic addressee is no longer named. The fix-instructions ('separating the prediction document from the observation document and timestamping each independently') can stay; that's substantive critique, which the canary is designed to surface.

holes_found.description: 'Single-sentence methodology error' is rhetorically loose and weakens the critique it summarizes. The OTS-wrong-side argument requires four structural components per the Timestamp Error tab's own scorecard (build-scripts/generate-html.js:1869-1879): (a) a predictions-only document, (b) timestamped before data collection, (c) a separate observations document, (d) timestamped after data collection, with (b) preceding (d). The full tab is ~800 lines of explanation precisely because the fix is structural, not a sentence-edit. Calling it a 'single-sentence methodology error' invites the dome defender's response 'if it's so simple why does the rest of the tab need 800 lines'. A reader who reads the rationale paragraph and then the scorecard immediately below will see the framing mismatch. The substance of the Timestamp Error critique is precisely that the FIX is structurally non-trivial — that's why it remains the canary even after the dome author wrote methodology.json conceding the distinction in prose without implementing the structural fix (per CLAUDE.md and the Breaking News block on the Overview tab).
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'single-sentence methodology error' with 'narrowly-scoped methodology error' or 'scoped methodology critique' in the tab-top ds-tab-rationale paragraph. The word 'narrow' or 'scoped' communicates the same 'this is unlike the dome's other problems' contrast (i.e., not a rebuild-the-whole-model fix) without inviting the rhetorical comeback. Bonus: aligns better with EXP-438's own internal language in anticipated_objections (the EXP itself says 'one file separation plus one extra OTS anchor — measured in hours, not in months') — the EXP knew the fix was scope-bounded but not single-sentence.

holes_found.description: 'The dome author can fix unilaterally' doesn't distinguish Timestamp Error from any other critique on this site. The justification for the standalone tab in the ds-tab-rationale paragraph is that the fix is unilateral. But the Self-Contradictions tab (where the dome math contradicts the dome's own values) is also unilaterally fixable by the dome author — every critique on this site is unilaterally fixable from the dome's side because it IS the dome's model. The actual distinguishing feature of Timestamp Error — that the fix is narrowly scoped and effort-bounded (hours not months, per EXP-438's own internal analysis) — would be a stronger argument. The current wording is technically true but conveys no information beyond 'this is one of the things the dome author could fix,' which is true of everything on the site. A future HOL-NARRATIVE reviewer evaluating tab-placement coherence will read this and correctly note the criterion 'unilaterally fixable' applies to all tabs, not just this one.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'is a single-sentence methodology error the dome author can fix unilaterally' with 'is a narrowly-scoped methodology error with a structurally simple fix' or 'is a scope-bounded methodology critique with a well-defined fix path'. Either form makes the distinguishing feature (narrow scope, simple structure) explicit instead of citing unilaterality which doesn't distinguish.

holes_found.description: First-person plural 'we' voice intrusion in the reading-guide paragraph. The pre-EXP-438 reading guide stays in third-person content-description mode throughout ('The Model covers the dome's stated methodology', 'Predictions Analysis audits the dome's registry'). The new clause 'the most actionable methodology critique we can hand the dome author, and we want it visible at the nav level' introduces editorial 'we' twice in one sentence — a voice the rest of the paragraph doesn't use. Other 'we' usages on the site are scoped to detailed argumentation in expanded prose, not the top-level reading guide which functions as a UX/navigation summary. The voice change is small but noticeable.
holes_found.recommendation: Bundled with the moderate-severity tone fix above. Switching to 'a narrow, separately-fixable methodology critique surfaced for visibility' (Option B from hole 1) removes both 'we' instances and preserves the placement rationale in third-person voice that matches the rest of the paragraph.

holes_found.description: The visible-rationale strategy is partly circular as a closure for HOL-NARRATIVE c2 hole 3. EXP-438's reasoning is that the rationale text will stop future curmudgeon reviews from re-flagging tab orphaning. But the rationale itself doesn't argue narrative coherence — it argues 'we want it visible at the nav level' (UX preference) and 'most actionable methodology critique' (strategic gift). Neither rebuts the underlying narrative-flow critique that the tab inserts a methodology argument between two falsification arguments (Kill Shots → Timestamp Error → Predictions Analysis). A future curmudgeon could correctly note: the placement is still narratively orphaned; the rationale just admits this and asserts the orphaning is intentional. A reviewer optimizing for narrative coherence still has grounds to flag — EXP-438 anticipates this in anticipated_objections item 3 ('A future curmudgeon review will still flag this... that is a curmudgeon prompt issue') but the response there hand-waves to 'the reading guide is the canonical narrative-structure document'. The visible-rationale strategy makes the placement defensible rather than coherent; that may be the best available trade-off given the canary constraint, but it's not the same thing as resolving the underlying narrative hole.
holes_found.recommendation: Acknowledge the trade-off explicitly in the EXP-438 closure record. The HOL-NARRATIVE c2 hole 3 is closed in the sense 'future reviewers will see the placement is intentional and read the rationale' but NOT closed in the sense 'the narrative flow is now coherent'. This is fine — the canary constraint forces the trade-off — but the closure ISS-2218 status note should reflect this distinction so a future maintainer reading the issue history doesn't assume the narrative-flow concern was substantively resolved.

stronger_arguments.description: EXP-438's anticipated_objections section item 1 addresses 'if the dome author has confirmed direct contact (CLAUDE.md 2026-04-27 note), canary-visibility-at-the-nav is no longer load-bearing — bundle the tab' with a response citing CLAUDE.md: 'The OTS canary still stands as the explicit fixed-prediction signal. Direct DM contact does not replace the canary — it adds a parallel confirmation channel.' That response is correct but incomplete. The stronger argument is that the canary is not load-bearing for THE AUTHOR (who is in DM contact and reading anyway), but it IS load-bearing for THIRD PARTIES — readers who come to this review via search or referral, who form their opinion of the dome model's scientific status partly from how prominently methodology issues are surfaced. Reducing canary visibility because we know the author is reading would optimize for one audience (the author) at the cost of another (the public). The EXP's argument as written reads 'the canary still applies'; the stronger argument is 'the canary serves two audiences and only one of them is in DM contact.'
sources: CLAUDE.md project context — The Canary (a.k.a. Honey Trap) section
CLAUDE.md project context — Author Contact Status (added 2026-04-27)

stronger_arguments.description: The reading-guide patch has a subtler value EXP-438 didn't emphasize: it serves as a stake-in-the-ground for the document's editorial voice — a place where 'we' speaks in first-person about UX choices. This may BE a feature, not a bug, IF the operator wants the Overview reading guide to take on more editorial voice. The pre-EXP guide is dry navigation; the post-EXP guide has one moment of editorial commentary, which could be read as a deliberate signal that this is a curated argument, not just a tab list. The hole 5 (minor) above flags this as a voice intrusion; but the operator may consider it a feature. Decider should consider whether 'we'-voice in the reading guide is desired or accidental — both the current state and the pre-EXP state are defensible.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-438's central decision is correct: KEEP Timestamp Error at tab position 8 with visible rationale rather than restructuring (EXP-438.json:decision.action). The supporting analysis is rigorous — the cross-reference inventory (EXP-438.json:analysis_of_tradeoff.cross_reference_substitution) is accurate (verified ~10 inline references from Overview hero, Reading Guide, Self-Contradictions §1582, External Tests §1751, Breaking News §1603, WIN-036, κ-cluster §6.14, AI & Conclusions, all confirmed in build-scripts/generate-html.js HEAD); the canary-active evidence is correct (methodology.json appearance + commit 6e06efb AI-Auditor block edit, both documented at build-scripts/generate-html.js:1646 Breaking News block); the trade-off analysis (test_cadence_break_assessment) correctly observes that tabs 4-7 are heterogeneous in argument shape, not a homogeneous test block. The two-patch fix (reading guide + tab-top rationale) is the minimum-disruption surface that closes the HOL-NARRATIVE c2 hole 3 visibility concern without restructuring. The decision to NOT combine Timestamp Error with Code Analysis into a 'Methodology Audit' tab is justified by the Self-Contradictions internal-arc argument (EXP-438.json:decision.why_not_combine_into_methodology_audit_tab). Avoiding the word 'canary' in the patch text is correct and follows CLAUDE.md guidance.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The implementation is partially compromised by the wording choices. Two moderate-severity tone holes (parallel-structure break in reading guide, canary motive telegraphed via 'we want it visible at the nav level' + 'methodology critique we can hand the dome author') and one moderate scope-mismatch hole ('single-sentence methodology error' undershoots what the fix entails) collectively weaken what is otherwise the right decision. The fix in each case is a minor word-swap, not a structural retreat — and EXP-438 itself used better internal language ('measured in hours, not in months', 'one file separation plus one extra OTS anchor', 'fix unilaterally' was contrasted with rebuild-the-model in the analyst's reasoning) that didn't make it into the public-facing patch text. The decider integrated the EXP's literal patch_text verbatim; the EXP's literal patch_text was the weakest version of the EXP's own reasoning.

deeper_analysis: Devils-advocate sweep — what would the dome author do with these two paragraphs if he reads them? He'd plausibly notice the editorial 'we want it visible at the nav level' and infer (correctly) that the placement is strategic. CLAUDE.md is explicit that direct DM contact has confirmed he is reading. The canary mechanism survives this — it never depended on him not knowing the tab was prominent; it depends on him not knowing the placement was specifically engineered to draw his attention to a fix-this-please error. The patches as integrated leak that one level deeper than the EXP's own analysis predicted: the EXP said the patches would not 'telegraph the canary mechanism'; the patches as written do telegraph the strategic motive, just not the word 'canary'. This is a graduated issue, not a binary failure. The substantive critique (OTS-wrong-side) is unchanged and still load-bearing; the canary's strategic-positioning aspect is now ~50% visible. The dome author's existing partial responses (methodology.json conceding the distinction in prose without the structural fix; AI-Auditor block edit) suggest he is more sensitive to the substantive argument than to the strategic framing — i.e., even if he reads 'we want it visible' he may not change behavior because his bottleneck is implementing the structural fix, not noticing it exists. Adversarial counterpoint: a sufficiently strategic dome-author response would be to NOT fix the structural error, because doing so confirms our review is being read and patched-against in real time. The visible-rationale wording mildly increases this risk by making the strategic gift-framing explicit. Net: the integration is right-direction-wrong-wording; the recommended_action is minor_edit, not major_rewrite, because the substantive decision (KEEP-AT-POSITION-8 with visible rationale) is correct and the wording is fixable with three small swaps. Across the four batched queue items (416-419) processed this run-batch by the decider, this is the integration most exposed to a future HOL-TONE flag — the others are placeholder-templating and arithmetic work where tone isn't the load surface; this one IS the tone.

citations_unchecked: Whether the dome author has actually engaged with the new rationale paragraph (no signal yet; would require a future poller change file referencing it)

summary_for_decider: EXP-438 integration landed correctly at the structural level — two prose patches matching the EXP's proposed_patches[0,1] verbatim, in the correct file locations (build-scripts/generate-html.js:1522 reading guide and :1866 tab-top), no link targets touched, no build-pipeline impact. Substantive decision (KEEP Timestamp Error at position 8 with visible rationale, do NOT combine with Code Analysis) is correct and well-justified by EXP-438's analysis. Six holes flagged at the wording level, none critical: 3 moderate (parallel-structure tone break in reading guide, canary motive telegraphed via 'we want it visible at the nav level' + 'methodology critique we can hand the dome author', 'single-sentence methodology error' undershoots the fix scope and invites a defender's comeback), 3 minor (unilaterality doesn't distinguish from other critiques, 'we'-voice intrusion in reading guide, visible-rationale strategy is partly circular vs HOL-NARRATIVE c2 hole 3). Recommended action: minor_edit. Three small word-swap follow-ups would close the moderate holes without re-routing through analyst: (1) reading-guide clause: replace 'most actionable methodology critique we can hand the dome author, and we want it visible at the nav level' with 'a narrow, separately-fixable methodology critique surfaced for visibility'; (2) tab-top rationale: replace 'single-sentence methodology error the dome author can fix unilaterally' with 'narrowly-scoped methodology error with a structurally simple fix path'; (3) optional follow-up note on ISS-2218 closure record acknowledging the trade-off (placement is defensible, not coherent — canary constraint forces this). Decider can self-apply (1) and (2) as a 3-line patch set; (3) is a one-line annotation. No verdict change needed; the substantive critique and decision are unchanged.
